title: OLAP Lakehouse: ADLS Gen2 + Synapse Serverless + Power BI
description: Azure-native lakehouse pattern centered on ADLS Gen2 for storage, Synapse serverless SQL for query-on-lake access, lake databases / shared metadata for cataloging, and Power BI for BI/reporting. Default posture emphasizes low-storage-cost analytics over data in the lake, with Power BI import or semantic caching used to deliver dashboard responsiveness that raw query-on-lake alone would not guarantee.

cost.costNotes: ADLS Gen2 storage is low-cost, Synapse serverless SQL is consumption-priced, and Power BI introduces user/capacity cost. This default posture is cheaper than a dedicated Synapse warehouse when storage volume is high and interactive serving needs are moderate.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min ($50): modest ADLS storage, low-volume serverless SQL scans, and limited Power BI footprint. Max ($7,000): larger analytical scans plus broader BI access and imported models. Still typically cheaper than always-on dedicated warehouse capacity for storage-heavy lake-first use cases.
provenance.adoptionCost: Adoption cost covers ADLS layout, Synapse serverless external tables/lake databases, metadata organization, and Power BI model/report integration. Estimate 12-15 developer days at ~$160/day = $2,400.
provenance.licenseCost: No separate software license beyond Azure and Power BI service consumption.
provenance.source: LLM analysis informed by Azure Synapse lake database documentation (as of 13-Apr-2026)

provides.reasoning: Synapse serverless SQL provides query-on-lake SQL access over ADLS Gen2 datasets.
Power BI is the default BI layer for curated analytics and dashboard delivery on top of the lakehouse.
Azure-native pipelines commonly land curated Parquet or Delta-style files in ADLS for lake-first analytics.
ADLS Gen2 is the primary analytical data plane and storage system for the lakehouse.
Synapse lake databases and shared metadata provide a cataloged view over data in the lake.

requires.reasoning: The lakehouse expects upstream batch or staged ingestion into ADLS and metadata refresh for queryable datasets.
ADLS, Synapse, and Power BI all rely on Azure identity and access control.

availability.reason: General architectural pattern applicable to Azure-native lakehouse workloads

supports_nfr.reason: Synapse serverless over ADLS is a query-on-lake posture and Power BI caching/import is needed to stay within the claimed dashboard latency envelope.
The default stack depends on ADLS, Synapse serverless SQL metadata/query layers, and Power BI refresh/serving, making three nines a defensible default target.
Recovery depends on storage, metadata, and BI refresh layers rather than an always-on warehouse cluster.
Freshness is bounded by ingestion cadence and metadata refresh rather than row-level transactional immediacy.
The default stack targets low-to-medium BI concurrency with imported/cached reporting rather than large-scale direct-query concurrency.
The lakehouse assumes batch or staged writes to ADLS-backed datasets and metadata updates, not high-frequency point writes.
Serverless SQL and metadata access over ADLS introduce non-trivial orchestration and startup overhead for lakehouse jobs.
Tail job starts are affected by serverless query startup, metadata refresh, and Power BI/import coordination.
The default Azure lakehouse posture assumes moderate batch and refresh frequency rather than very high job churn.
Azure identity and Power BI access controls make the stack compatible with multiple application auth choices.
Azure provides auditability across storage, Synapse, and BI layers.
PII can be handled with storage access control, Entra-based permissions, and BI governance.
GDPR compatibility depends on governance, residency, and deletion workflows rather than the platform choice alone.
HIPAA compatibility depends on encryption, access control, and operational safeguards.
CCPA compatibility depends on data discovery and deletion workflows across the lakehouse.
SOX compatibility depends on access control, auditability, and reporting governance.
ADLS-backed datasets can support strict durability when configured appropriately.

supports_constraints.reason: Azure lakehouse access is SQL- and API-driven and does not constrain the application language.
Azure lakehouse can serve the analytical plane behind web, mobile, desktop, and API products while also supporting worker, batch, and data-pipeline execution.
Primary gate: this variant is for OLAP analytical workloads.

storage_layer.description: Default analytical storage layer. ADLS Gen2 is the source of truth for the Azure lakehouse.

query_engine.description: Serverless SQL is the default because it keeps the default stack aligned with lake-first, pay-per-query economics.

metadata_catalog.description: Lake databases are the default operational catalog. Add Purview when stronger enterprise cataloging and governance are required.

table_format.description: Parquet is the conservative default for broad Synapse serverless interoperability. Delta is an alternative when Spark-oriented lakehouse workflows dominate.

bi_layer.description: Power BI import is the default because it better matches the claimed dashboard latency envelope than pure direct-query over the lake.

ingestion_mode.description: Default ingestion lands curated analytical files in ADLS. Spark-oriented Delta writes are an alternative when the lakehouse is more Spark-centric.
